one_line_takeaway: TimeMark achieves cryptographically-motivated, provably-argued perfect (800/800) generation-time recovery from watermarked LLM text by moving the timestamp into an HSM-guarded, one-way-evolving secret key rather than the watermark payload itself, using a random non-reused payload plus BCH coding to block the provider-backdating and statistical-forgery attacks that undermine prior multi-bit LLM watermarks -- at the cost of requiring long (~945-token) generations and no built-in resistance to text editing.
key_finding: Empirically (Table 2, the paper's own results, not cited from prior work): all 800/800 (100%) watermarked texts had their generation time correctly identified, and 0/800 (0%) non-watermarked texts were falsely identified as carrying a generation time. The average Step-2 verification score was 0.7699 for watermarked texts versus 0.4993 for non-watermarked texts, a clear separation around the phi=0.65 threshold. Theoretically, under the independence approximation with delta=2.5, alpha=5, L=945, the derived probability of correctly recovering R in Step 1 is pR ~ 1 - 6.40e-34 (or ~1 - 1.76e-19 under the more conservative 35%-greenlist-mass assumption); Step 2 false-rejection probability for the correct (K,R) is ~1.61e-44 and false-acceptance probability for an incorrect (K,R) is ~4.76e-8 at threshold phi=0.65.
core_contribution: TimeMark, a multi-bit LLM watermarking framework whose own novel contribution (per the paper's Introduction and Section 5, not the Related Work) is to decouple the target information (generation timestamp) from the watermark payload. Instead of encoding the timestamp directly as a multi-bit payload (as prior multi-bit watermarking work does), TimeMark binds the timestamp to a time-evolving secret key K_t = H(K_t-1) derived via a one-way hash chain and held by an external Hardware Security Module (HSM) under regulatory supervision, while the actual embedded payload is a freshly-generated random bit sequence R that is never stored. A two-stage encoding scheme (Stage I uses R in the pseudo-random greenlist seed; Stage II omits R) plus BCH error-correcting coding lets a verifier recover R and, separately, verify which candidate time-key Kt produced the text, yielding what the authors formally define (Definition 1) as a 'trustworthy watermark' satisfying Correctness, provider-side Unforgeability, and user-side Unforgeability.
threat_model: Two distinct adversaries are defined in Section 4 (Definition 1) and motivated in Sections 1-3: (1) Provider-side forgery -- the LLM service provider itself, which controls the encoding process, could otherwise backdate or postdate generated text by reusing an old watermark key; TimeMark blocks this by making historical keys irrecoverable to the provider (only the HSM under an auditing regime retains the one-way key-evolution history). (2) User-side statistical/spoofing attack -- an adversary with black-box query access to the watermarked LLM (knowing the timestamp-to-bit encoding rule and bit-allocation rule but not the secret key) collects watermarked documents, trains a surrogate classifier h(y|c,v) mapping context-token pairs to inferred payload bits, and uses the resulting surrogate score to bias sampling toward a target forged timestamp t*. The paper cites this exact attack pattern from Jovanovic et al. (2024), reporting that in that prior work an adversary needed about 30,000 API queries to learn a surrogate watermark function and achieved over 80% average forgery success rate against conventional multi-bit watermarks. TimeMark's random, non-reused payload is designed to make this specific statistical-imitation attack ineffective because there is no stable payload-to-token statistical pattern to learn across generations. The paper explicitly frames spoofing/forgery, not scrubbing (paraphrase/edit removal) attacks, as the primary threat for the judicial-evidence use case, arguing that removal only causes missing evidence whereas forgery produces false evidence.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Conclusion, Section 7): (1) the scheme currently requires long-text generation (L>=945 tokens in experiments) to guarantee near-perfect detection accuracy -- it does not yet support short texts; (2) the framework assumes the user preserves the AIGC output without manual modification, i.e., it does not claim or evaluate robustness against text editing/paraphrasing/scrubbing attacks -- the authors explicitly deprioritize this in favor of the spoofing/forgery threat. Additionally noted internally: the theoretical analysis relies on a standard independence approximation between token-level decoding outcomes that the authors acknowledge does not strictly hold for LLM outputs, and the greenlist-token-probability-mass assumption (50%) is a simplification the authors themselves flag may deviate substantially in practice, stating the Step 1 recovery accuracy 'still needs to be further validated through real-data experiments' beyond the theoretical estimate. The empirical evaluation is limited to a single model (Qwen2.5-7B) and a fixed prompt pool of 20 prompts across two content types (creative generation, report writing); no evaluation is reported against the surrogate-classifier forgery attack itself (it is discussed only via a prior paper's reported numbers, not replicated against TimeMark), no comparison against a baseline multi-bit watermarking method's accuracy is run empirically (the accuracy contrast with prior methods is argued via the literature/theory rather than a head-to-head experiment), and no key-compromise or HSM-failure scenario is evaluated.

datasets_used: Self-constructed pool of 20 prompts covering creative generation and report writing tasks (no external benchmark dataset used)
quantitative_results: 800/800 (100%) watermarked texts correctly identified as to generation time
0/800 (0%) non-watermarked texts falsely identified as having a generation time
Average Step-2 verification score for watermarked texts: 0.7699
Average Step-2 verification score for non-watermarked texts: 0.4993
Theoretical bit-decode probability p_bit ≈ 0.9995427 (bit error q_bit ≈ 0.0004573) under delta=2.5
Theoretical R-recovery probability p_R ≈ 1 - 6.40e-34 (50% greenlist-mass assumption) or ≈ 1 - 1.76e-19 (35% greenlist-mass assumption, based on Kirchenbauer et al. 2023 data)
Theoretical Step-2 false-rejection probability ≈ 1.61e-44; false-acceptance probability ≈ 4.76e-8, at threshold phi=0.65
BCH(63,10) code: minimum distance d_min=27, correcting up to t=13 bit errors

Configuration: Qwen2.5-7B model, R=10 bits, BCH-expanded payload=63 bits, alpha=5 (315 Stage-I tokens), L>=945 generated tokens, bias delta=2.5, verification threshold phi=0.65, 5 candidate keys searched per decode
baselines_compared: No empirical head-to-head baseline comparison is run in this paper. Prior methods are discussed only qualitatively/theoretically in Related Work and Preliminaries: Kirchenbauer et al. (2023) greenlist/redlist zero-bit watermarking; Dathathri et al. (2024) SynthID-Text; Yoo et al. (2024) position-allocation multi-bit watermarking; Wang et al. (2024) codable multi-bit watermarking; Jovanovic et al. (2024) statistical spoofing attack (cited figures: ~30,000 API queries, >80% average forgery success rate against conventional multi-bit watermarks -- these are the prior paper's own reported numbers, not reproduced by this paper's authors); Fairoze et al. (2025) publicly detectable/cryptographically signed watermarking (discussed as lacking a time-binding mechanism)
